title: Ax-Schanuel for Shimura Varieties
speaker: Jacob Tsimerman
abstract: Abstract: (joint with N.Mok, J.Pila) Shimura varieties (S) are uniformized by symmetric spaces (H), and the uniformization map Pi:H --> S is quite transcendental. Understanding the interaction of this map with the two algebraic structures is of particular interest in arithmetic, as it is a necessary ingredient for the modern approaches to the Andre-Oort and Zilber-Pink conjectures, as well As a new approac to Shafarevich type theorems about integral points on varieties.

proof_stages.label: Holomorphic uniformization framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a holomorphic, typically non-algebraic map \(\pi:\Omega\to X\) between spaces carrying complex algebraic structures, with the exponential map, the modular \(j\)-map, and general Shimura uniformizations as guiding cases.

proof_stages.label: Hierarchy of algebraic interaction statements
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates three increasing levels of interaction—classification of bi-algebraic sets, Ax-Lindemann containment, and Ax-Schanuel unlikely-intersection statements—and recalls their known status in the exponential, modular, and Shimura settings.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic analogue and motivation
proof_stages.summary: Bi-algebraic points are related to arithmetic special points such as roots of unity and CM points, motivating the geometric Ax-Schanuel picture as a prerequisite for arithmetic unlikely-intersection problems.

proof_stages.label: Pila-style counting strategy for Ax-Schanuel
proof_stages.summary: Starting from one supposed unlikely intersection, monodromy supplies many translates; after restriction to a definable fundamental domain, a definable set of bad group elements contains many integer points, so Pila-Wilkie produces semi-algebraic curves of bad parameters.

proof_stages.label: Induction and the stabilizer obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The semi-algebraic curve of bad translates should enlarge \(V\) and drive an induction, but the argument stalls when \(V\) is stabilized by a nontrivial subgroup, especially in higher-rank nonabelian Shimura groups.

proof_stages.label: Mok's algebraic foliation via jet spaces
proof_stages.summary: Mok's idea replaces individual transcendental images of algebraic varieties by algebraic differential data: a \(G_\mathbb C\)-invariant algebraic family upstairs defines an algebraic subvariety of finite jet space downstairs, with algebraicity proved using Chow-type/definable Chow arguments.

proof_stages.label: Algebraicity of bad loci and final reduction
proof_stages.summary: The jet-space construction implies that the locus in \(W\) where some member of the family has too-large intersection with \(\pi^{-1}W\) is algebraic, producing many bad intersections without relying solely on monodromy and allowing the Pila framework to reduce to normal stabilizers and quotients.

examples.name: Exponential map prototype
examples.note: The map \(z\mapsto e^{2\pi i z}\) from \(\mathbb C^n\) to \((\mathbb C^*)^n\) is used as the classical prototype for bi-algebraic sets, Ax-Lindemann/Ax-Schanuel statements, and the comparatively easy abelian stabilizer situation.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker develops the exponential prototype by writing the map, using addition-to-multiplication, and characterizing its bi-algebraic sets as rational affine subspaces/cosets

examples.name: Definability of the \(j\)-function via its \(q\)-expansion
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that on a fundamental domain the \(q\)-expansion of \(j\) is built from restricted sine/cosine, the real exponential, and a convergent power series, illustrating definability in \(\mathbb R_{\mathrm{an},\exp}\).
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker verifies definability of the j-function by decomposing its q-expansion into real exponential, bounded sine/cosine, and a convergent power series on the fundamental domain

examples.name: The \(\mathrm{SL}_2\) stabilizer cases
examples.note: The scarcity of subgroups of \(\mathrm{SL}_2\)—essentially tori and unipotent groups—is used to illustrate why the earlier \(j\)-case proof could be completed ad hoc but did not generalize to higher-rank Shimura varieties.
